---
title: "PSTAT 134 Final Project Report"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Introduction

The NBA playoffs represent the highest level of competition in professional basketball, where teams compete in a series of elimination rounds to determine the league champ. For our analysis we decided to take a look at historical NBA regular-season level data and see how different performance metrics (offensive and defensive) influence a teams' success in the playoffs. With the increasing availability of advanced basketball statistics, it is now more possible than ever to examine which team characteristics are most strongly associated with success in the playoffs. The purpose of this analysis is to identify key factors of success in the playoffs, as well as use those factors to develop a model capable of predicting the winner of a playoff series given the relevant teams' performance in the regular season.

## Methods

### Exploratory Data Analysis / Feature Engineering

To identify which regular season team statistics were most strongly associated with playoff success, an exploratory data analysis was conducted using NBA playoff team data from the 2015-16 through 2024-25 seasons. This ten-year window was selected to provide a sufficient sample size while remaining relevant to the modern era of NBA play, particularly the increased emphasis on three-point shooting and offensive efficiency.

Ten regular season team performance metrics were selected based on their prevalence in basketball analytics literature: Win Percentage, Net Rating, Offensive Rating, Defensive Rating, Simple Rating System (SRS), Effective Field Goal Percentage (eFG%), True Shooting Percentage (TS%), Three-Point Attempt Rate (3PAr), Turnover Percentage (TOV%), and Offensive Rebound Percentage (ORB%).

Rather than using raw team statistics, feature engineering was performed to create matchup-level variables by computing the difference between the two competing teams for each metric. For example, instead of using TS% for a single team, TS% differential was used to capture the relative efficiency advantage between opponents. Additional features were engineered including Strength of Schedule, Simple Rating System, and head-to-head metrics such as win percentage and shooting efficiency differentials.

### Correlation Analysis

Pearson correlation coefficients were then calculated to quantify the linear relationship between each engineered feature and playoff series outcomes. This was done in two stages: first examining raw regular season metrics against playoff win percentage, then evaluating matchup-difference variables against series winners. Pearson correlation was chosen for its interpretability and its utility as a first step in identifying promising predictors for downstream modeling.

![](top10_predictors.png)

As shown by the chart, the top five characteristics in terms of pearson correlation to playoff series deciders include: Win%, Effective Field Goal%, True Shooting%, Net rating, and Plus-Minus (all of which are differential statistics between two teams). All of these metrics are somewhat similar in the sense that they describe a teams' offensive capabilities and their ability to score (Net rating describes both offensive and defensive skill). The Simple Rating System (SRS) metric is an advanced statistic that measures a teams' overall strength by combining their average point differential (Plus-Minus), with their Strength of Schedule (a metric that takes into account the average rating of each opponent faced). As shown by the plot, the Plus-Minus and SRS differential characteristics posses very similar pearson correlation values, indicating that the Strength of Schedule metric, adds little predictive power to a playoff series outcome.

### Chosen Machine Learning Models

To transition from exploratory correlation analysis to predictive modeling, we selected two distinct classification frameworks capable of handling continuous, differential team statistics to predict binary series outcomes ($Y \in \{0, 1\}$, where 1 represents a series win for Team A).

#### Elastic Net Logistic Regression

Logistic regression is a foundational classification algorithm that models the probability of a binary outcome using the logistic function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(Y=1|X)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-(\beta_0+\beta_1X_1+...+\beta_pX_p)}}
\end{aligned}
$$ Because many of our engineered feature differentials (such as Effective Field Goal Percentage, True Shooting Percentage, and Net Rating) exhibit strong multicollinearity, a standard ordinary least squares or unpenalized logistic approach would suffer from high variance and unstable coefficient estimates. To mitigate this, we utilized Elastic Net Regularization, which linearly combines the penalties of both Ridge ($L_2$) and Lasso ($L_1$) regression. The objective function minimizes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\beta_0, \beta} \left[ -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N y_i \cdot \log(p_i) + (1-y_i) \cdot \log(1-p_i) \right] + \lambda \left( \frac{1-\alpha}{2} \|\beta\|_2^2 + \alpha \|\beta\|_1 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

-   The Lasso Penalty ($\alpha = 1$): Performs automatic feature selection by driving less impactful coefficients exactly to zero, which helps eliminate redundant metrics.

-   The Ridge Penalty ($\alpha = 0$): Handles highly correlated predictors by shrinking their coefficients toward each other, ensuring stable weights for overlapping efficiency metrics.By tuning the blending hyperparameter ($\alpha$) and the regularization penalty strength ($\lambda$) via cross-validation, the model parsimoniously selects the most critical regular-season success indicators while penalizing over-complexity.

#### Random Forest

While logistic regression assumes a linear relationship between the log-odds of winning and the team differentials, postseason basketball often features non-linear thresholds where marginal advantages scale unpredictably. To capture these interactions, we implemented a Random Forest Classifier, an ensemble learning method constructed from a multitude of independent decision trees.

The algorithm operates on the principle of bootstrap aggregation (bagging) and feature randomness:

1.  Resampling: Multiple bootstrap samples are drawn from the historical training data.

2.  Tree Construction: A deep decision tree is grown for each sample. At each node split, the algorithm only considers a random subset of the engineered predictors (determined by the mtry hyperparameter), preventing dominant features like raw Win Percentage from masking subtle, complex interactions between shooting efficiency and rebounding metrics.

3.  Ensemble Voting: The final prediction is determined by a majority vote across all constructed trees, which drastically reduces model variance and protects against overfitting.

This non-parametric approach naturally handles multi-collinear inputs and is ideally suited for discovering complex, conditional relationships—such as how an offensive rebounding advantage might only become predictive when a team's true shooting differential is negative.

## Results

### Model Performance and Evaluation

To determine the most robust predictive framework for the NBA playoffs, we evaluated both an Elastic Net Logistic Regression and a Random Forest classifier. Performance was assessed using accuracy and the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (`roc_auc`). Because playoff series outcomes are fundamentally driven by probabilistic edges where a team must win four out of seven games, we prioritized `roc_auc` to measure how effectively each model separates historical winners from losers across varying decision thresholds.

The Random Forest model demonstrated superior predictive power, achieving a higher `roc_auc` than the Elastic Net Logistic Regression. While logistic regression struggles with the highly correlated, multi-collinear nature of team basketball metrics, the Random Forest model naturally accommodated non-linear relationships and captured distinct playoff intensity thresholds.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-model-compare
#| tbl-cap: "Model Performance Comparison"
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(ggplot2)

model_compare <- tibble(
  Model = c("Elastic Net Logistic Regression", "Random Forest"),
  Accuracy = c(0.714, 0.643),  
  `ROC AUC` = c(0.844, 0.889)  
)

model_compare %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "Postseason Predictive Performance",
    subtitle = "Comparison of classification frameworks"
  ) %>%
  cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = everything()
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_body(
      columns = c(`ROC AUC`),
      rows = Model == "Random Forest"
    )
  ) %>%
  opt_interactive(active = FALSE)

model_compare %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Accuracy, `ROC AUC`), names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Model, y = Value, fill = Model)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~Metric) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(y = "Score", x = NULL, title = "Model Performance Breakdown")
```

### Feature Importance

![](logreg_imp_top10.png)

As shown by the plot, the logistic regression model selected only eight of the predictors included in the recipe. Among the top five of those include: Win%, True-shooting%, Effective Field-Goal%, and Offensive/Net rating (all of which are differentials between Team A and Team B). This means that the model prioritizes teams with high regular season win/loss records (to be expected), as well as efficient shooting, more so than it does defensive metrics. These results highlight the distinct shift the NBA has taken in the modern era, putting a heavy importance on a teams' ability to score rather than defend. Interestingly enough, the model also selected the head-to-head Win% differential statistic, implying that a teams' regular season win/loss record against a specific team yields predictive power in the outcome of a series matchup in the playoffs.

![](rf_imp_top10.png)

The Random Forest yields somewhat similar relevant features. Among the top five of those include: Win%, Effective Field-Goal%, True-Shooting%, Plus-Minus, and Net rating (once again, all of which are differentials between Team A and B). We can see that both models yield the same top three predictors yet in slight different orders; the random forest model prioritizes Effective Field-Goal% over True-Shooting%. Although these differences are slight, this means that the logistic regression model puts a heavier emphasis on points generated via the free-throw (included in ts%), than the random forest model does.

### Predictions on Test Data (2026 Playoffs) - Random Forest

```{r, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
library(dplyr)
library(kableExtra)

rf_series_predictions <- read.csv("rf_series_predictions.csv")

rf_finals_predictions <- read.csv("rf_finals_predictions.csv")

rf_series_predictions %>%
  mutate(Correct = NULL, Confidence = NULL) %>%  # hide flag column in display
  kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "RF NBA Playoff Predictions Sorted by Confidence (Green = Correct)"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    font_size = 12
  ) %>%
  row_spec(
    which(rf_series_predictions$Correct),
    background = "#d4f7d4"  # light green
  )
```

As shown by the table above and as indicated previously, the Random Forest Model has a test-accuracy of `64.3%`, which corresponded to nine correct predictions out of the 14 completed series of the 2026 playoffs. The table, sorted by model confidence, shows that the series in which the model was most confident in its outcome correlated to lopsided real-life results in the playoffs. This includes the Knicks sweep against the 76ers (in which the model predicted their win probability at 91.5%), the Spurs 4-1 victory over the Trailblazers (87.6%), and the Thunder's sweep against the Suns (81.1%). One of the most interesting correct series predictions from the random forest model was the Cavaliers over the Pistons in the Eastern Conference Semifinals. It is one of only three predictions in which the model predicted the lower seeded team (indicated by parentheses), to take the series, and it did so with relatively high confidence (60.4%). This prediction contrasted real-world trading odds, which favored the pistons at roughly 57%. For fun, the table below shows our models' prediction and confidence for Knicks Spurs NBA finals matchup.

```{r, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
rf_finals_predictions %>%
  kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "2026 NBA Finals: Random Forest Model Prediction"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    font_size = 13
  ) %>%
  row_spec(
    1, 
    bold = TRUE, 
    background = "#FFF2CC" 
  )

```

## Conclusion

This project examined whether regular-season team statistics could be used to predict NBA playoff success and playoff series winners. Through exploratory data analysis, we found that season-long measures of team quality were consistently more predictive of playoff performance than head-to-head matchup statistics. In particular, win percentage, true shooting percentage, effective field goal percentage, net rating, and offensive rating emerged as the strongest indicators of playoff success.

To evaluate the predictive power of these metrics, we developed both Random Forest and Elastic Net Logistic Regression models using matchup-based feature differences between teams. Both models demonstrated strong predictive performance, with the Logistic Regression model achieving an accuracy of 71.4% and the Random Forest model achieving an ROC AUC of 88.9%. Although the Logistic Regression model produced slightly higher accuracy, the Random Forest model achieved superior discrimination ability as measured by AUC, making it the stronger overall model for this application.

Overall, the results suggest that advanced efficiency metrics and season-long team performance provide valuable information for forecasting NBA playoff outcomes. While no model can perfectly predict the uncertainty of postseason basketball, our findings demonstrate that regular-season statistics can be used to successfully identify playoff contenders and predict series winners with a relatively high degree of accuracy. Future research could incorporate player-level data, injury information, betting market odds, or game-by-game playoff performance metrics to further improve predictive accuracy and better capture factors that influence playoff success.
